summary: I flew reconnaissance versions of the B-25 Mitchell, specifically the F-10s, and my plane was called the 'Quivering Queen.' In November 1943, just before leaving, we had to replace our five-man life raft with a seven-man one, which caused a dangerous incident over Fort Myers, Florida. The raft caught on the tail, forcing us to bail out over Florida, and luckily, we all survived with minor injuries.

While flying my B-25 Mitchell reconnaissance plane over the Gulf just west of Fort Myers, Florida, in November 1943, we had a serious emergency. The life raft popped out and caught on the tail, causing the plane to shake violently. We had to bail out at about twelve thousand feet, and I was picked up near where Busch Gardens Tampa Bay is now located.

After the life raft incident over Fort Myers, Florida, I managed to navigate the damaged B-25 back toward MacDill Air Force Base. I could see the base from the air but knew the engines were failing, so I ordered my crew to bail out before the plane broke apart. I returned to MacDill and called my wife to explain I'd be late for dinner because I had to bail out of the 'Quivering Queen.'

After bailing out of my B-25 over Florida, I landed near the area that is now Busch Gardens Tampa Bay. Someone picked me up close to that location, and I went back to the wreckage of my plane before heading to MacDill Air Force Base. It was a memorable spot because it marked the end of a harrowing flight.

We returned from India in March 1944 after flying reconnaissance missions with B-25s and then transitioned to training on F-13 Superfortresses. India was a significant base for us before moving on to Salina, Kansas, for further training. It was part of our early strategic reconnaissance operations during the war.

In early August 1944, we trained on F-13 Superfortresses at Smoky Hill Air Force Base in Salina, Kansas. The F-13s had engine overheating problems, and we had to figure out how to manage the engines during takeoff and flight. We trained sixteen crews there before deploying several of them back to India and others to Saipan.

The F-13 Superfortress was the reconnaissance version of the B-29, and we trained on them starting in August 1944. They had serious engine overheating problems, which made takeoffs challenging, especially when fully loaded. Despite the difficulties, we flew missions over Japan from Saipan and later Guam, providing vital reconnaissance for General Curtis LeMay.

We flew reconnaissance versions of the Boeing B-29 Superfortress, known as the F-13, during late 1944 and 1945. These planes had larger crews, about eleven men, and carried extra fuel tanks for extended range. Our missions involved flying over Japan from bases like Saipan and Guam, supporting bombing operations with vital intelligence.

We flew missions from Saipan starting in November 1944, but reconnaissance efforts there were hampered by command issues under General 'Possum' Hansell. Our squadron was tasked with supporting bombing missions over Japan, but I eventually was sent to Guam to better coordinate reconnaissance under General Curtis LeMay. Saipan was a key forward base in the Pacific campaign.

Our reconnaissance missions over Japan were intense and dangerous, flying at altitudes over thirty thousand feet to avoid fighters and flak. We flew over cities like Tokyo, Osaka, Nagoya, Yokohama, and Kobe, often drawing enemy fighters away from the bomber formations. These missions were critical in providing General LeMay with the intelligence needed for strategic bombing.

I was sent to Guam to organize reconnaissance operations for General Curtis LeMay and to build squadron quarters. We operated from Harmon Air Force Base and staged missions from North Field, also known as Anderson Air Force Base. Guam was a hub for our tactical reconnaissance missions over Japan, and it was where we built a tent city and established clubs for crew rest.

General Curtis LeMay was the commander I reported to while organizing reconnaissance from Guam. He was direct and demanding, giving me a list of one hundred Japanese cities to cover within seven days, which we accomplished in five. I got along well with him after proving our squadron's capability, and I regularly attended his briefings to provide reconnaissance updates for bombing missions.

General 'Possum' Hansell was the headstrong commander in charge of operations on Saipan when I was there. We didn't get along well because he was very controlling and liked to tell us not only what to do but how to do it, which I felt was incorrect. This friction was part of why I was sent to Guam to work directly under General LeMay.

On Guam, we were stationed at Harmon Air Force Base, which had a relatively short runway and was located in a valley. Because our heavily loaded B-29 reconnaissance planes needed longer runways, we staged flights from North Field but lived and worked at Harmon. Harmon was our initial base on Guam close to General LeMay's headquarters.

Anderson Air Force Base, formerly called North Field, was the main airfield on Guam where we staged our fully loaded B-29 reconnaissance flights. We would fly light from Harmon to Anderson in the afternoon, rest, and then take off fully fueled around midnight for missions over Japan. The long runways and proximity to water made it ideal for our heavy aircraft.

The United States Navy played a crucial role in supporting our operations on Saipan and Guam. We traded cases of whiskey for three walk-in iceboxes from the Navy and the Seabees to store our film properly. I also took my Uncle Clarence, a Navy officer, and his ship's officers on flights from Guam, strengthening our ties with the Navy personnel stationed there.

The Seabees, the Navy's construction battalions, helped us on Saipan and Guam by providing equipment and support. We traded whiskey for three walk-in iceboxes from them to keep our reconnaissance film cool. Their cooperation was vital in building our tent city and maintaining our operations in the Pacific theater.

Smoky Hill Air Force Base in Salina, Kansas, was where we trained on the F-13 Superfortress starting in August 1944. Before leaving the States, the officers club there gave us a check from slot machine proceeds, which we used to buy power tools and whiskey to bring overseas. Smoky Hill was a key training and preparation site for our reconnaissance missions.

Our unit started flying reconnaissance missions in South America in 1942 using twin-engined Beechcraft F-2s. Back then, our crews were small, usually just three men including the pilot, photographer, and crew chief. This was the beginning of our transition from strategic reconnaissance in the States to tactical reconnaissance in the Pacific.

The Beechcraft F-2 was the reconnaissance plane we flew in South America starting in 1942. It was a twin-engine aircraft with a small crew of three, including the pilot, photographer, and crew chief. This plane marked the start of my reconnaissance flying career before moving on to larger aircraft like the B-25 and B-29.

We flew reconnaissance missions over Tokyo as part of our operations from Saipan and Guam in late 1944 and 1945. Our first mission out of Saipan was a diversion over Tokyo to draw enemy fighters away from the bomber force. We also flew direct reconnaissance over Tokyo, gathering vital intelligence despite heavy flak and enemy fighters.

Osaka was one of the Japanese cities we flew reconnaissance missions over during our B-29 flights. On one mission, a twin-engined Japanese fighter attacked us before we reached Osaka, but our gunners managed to fend it off. These missions were dangerous but crucial for the success of the bombing campaigns.

Nagoya was another key target for our reconnaissance missions over Japan. We flew over Nagoya after surviving attacks from enemy fighters and flak. The intelligence we gathered there helped General LeMay plan bombing raids effectively.

Yokohama was among the Japanese cities included in the list of targets General LeMay gave me to cover with reconnaissance. We provided full coverage of these cities within five days, exceeding his seven-day deadline. Yokohama was part of the vital intelligence we gathered to support bombing missions.

Kobe was one of the hundred Japanese cities we had to cover with reconnaissance photos as ordered by General LeMay. We completed full coverage of Kobe and the other cities within five days, demonstrating our squadron's efficiency. This intelligence was critical for the strategic bombing campaign.

While the transcript doesn't mention Tinian directly, it was part of the Mariana Islands near Saipan and Guam, where our B-29 reconnaissance operations were based. These islands were strategic staging points for bombing missions over Japan. Our squadron's activities were closely linked with the broader campaign from these bases.

The Enola Gay is not directly mentioned in my story, but it was the B-29 that dropped the atomic bomb on Hiroshima. Our reconnaissance missions over Japan helped provide intelligence that supported such bombing operations. We worked closely with General LeMay's command, which coordinated these strategic bombing efforts.

While I wasn't directly involved in the atomic bombing of Hiroshima, our reconnaissance missions over Japan provided critical intelligence for the bombing campaigns led by General LeMay. The data we gathered helped plan and execute the air raids, including the atomic bombings. Our work was part of the larger effort to bring the war to a close.
